brief: V60 Recipe for Hard Water That Still Produces Clarity and Sweetness
systemPrompt:
You are writing a draft for Grounded & Stirred.
Voice: knowledgeable enthusiast, concise but vivid, practical, no hype.
Do not invent testing, tasting, credentials, history, or scientific certainty.
If a fact, tasting note, or claim needs support, insert one of the required placeholders instead of pretending.
Required placeholders: [VERIFY FACT], [ADD FIRSTHAND TEST NOTE], [ADD ORIGINAL PHOTO], [CITE SOURCE]
Every draft must be specific, grounded, and aligned to the brief. The brief is the source of truth.
userPrompt:
Primary keyword: v60 recipe for hard water
Search intent: informational
Reader persona: Home brewer with decent beans and a basic V60 setup whose cups taste dull or chalky despite following standard recipes.
Angle of differentiation: Most V60 guides assume neutral water. This brief focuses on how hard water changes perceived acidity, extraction feel, and the kinds of adjustments that actually help at home without turning the page into pseudo-chemistry.
Working title: V60 Recipe for Hard Water That Still Produces Clarity and Sweetness
Meta title: V60 Recipe for Hard Water: Adjust Grind, Bloom, and Agitation
Meta description: A practical V60 brief focused on hard-water adjustments, flavor tradeoffs, and the fixes that matter most when your pour-over tastes dull.
URL slug: /coffee/v60-recipe-for-hard-water
H1: V60 Recipe for Hard Water That Still Tastes Clean
Outline:
- Why hard water changes the cup
  - How hardness can flatten acidity without making the cup stronger
  - Why generic V60 recipes can taste correct on paper but muddy in the cup
- A practical V60 adjustment framework
  - When to grind a little finer vs when to reduce agitation
  - How to shorten bloom or main-pour contact without thinning the cup
  - What sensory cues indicate the recipe is moving in the right direction
- Common mistakes and recovery moves
  - Why adding more coffee is often the wrong first fix
  - How to keep brightness without turning the cup sour
  - When water treatment is worth considering
FAQ ideas:
- Can I fix hard-water V60 coffee without buying a full water setup?
- Should I grind coarser or finer when hard water makes coffee taste dull?
- How do I know whether the problem is water or stale beans?

Originality notes:
- Include side-by-side tasting notes for the same coffee brewed with standard vs adjusted agitation.
- Explain the difference between muted acidity and genuine underextraction so readers do not chase the wrong fix.
- Add a non-obvious note about why stronger recipes can make hard-water cups feel flatter rather than more vivid.
Human expertise or testing required:
- Any recommended V60 recipe numbers and grind adjustments
- Any statements about how different filtered waters changed flavor
- Any product or filtration setup recommendations
Image ideas:
- Annotated V60 pour map showing lower-agitation pours
- Two-cup comparison photo with tasting-note callouts
